title: Feature Engineering & Feature Selection
description: The art and science of creating and selecting features: encoding, scaling, handling missing data, feature crosses, and selection methods with FAANG-depth intuition.
details: Feature engineering is the most impactful step in the ML pipeline — better features beat better algorithms. This file covers the complete toolkit: encoding categorical variables (one-hot, label, target, count, hash), handling missing data (imputation strategies), feature scaling (standardization, normalization, robust scaling), feature crosses and interaction features, numerical transformations (polynomial features, binning, basis expansion), time-based and lag features, text features (TF-IDF, count vectors, n-grams), and feature selection methods (filter, wrapper, embedded). Every technique here is fair game in FAANG ML interviews at all levels.

sections.title: Categorical Encoding
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: ML models need numbers, not strings. The choice of encoding determines whether the model can exploit the structure in categorical variables.

Encoding types:

  • One-Hot Encoding: k categories → k binary columns. Use for low cardinality (< 10) with no ordinal relationship. Dummy trap: drop one column to avoid perfect multicollinearity.
  • Label Encoding: Each category gets an arbitrary integer (e.g., red=0, blue=1, green=2). Dangerous — implies ordinal relationship. Use only for tree-based models (they handle arbitrary integers via splits).
  • Target Encoding: Replace category with the mean of the target for that category. Powerful but leaks information — must use cross-validation to prevent data leakage. Regularize with global mean smoothing.
  • Count Encoding: Replace category with its frequency in the training set. Simple, no leakage, works well with tree models. Can cause collisions if many categories have similar counts.
  • Hash Encoding: Hash category to a fixed number of dimensions. Useful for high-cardinality features. Collisions are possible but often harmless with enough hash space.

[Practical Workflow] Decision rule: ≤ 5 categories → One-Hot. 5-50 categories → Target (with CV). > 50 categories → Count, Hash, or learn entity embeddings.
sections.codeBlock:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from category_encoders import TargetEncoder
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, LabelEncoder

# === One-Hot ===
ohe = OneHotEncoder(drop='first', sparse_output=False)
encoded = ohe.fit_transform(df[['color']])
# Get column names
colors = ohe.get_feature_names_out(['color'])

# === Target Encoding (CV-safe) ===
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold

def target_encode(df, col, target):
    ",".join(target_encode.__doc__ or [])
    kf = KFold(5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
    encoded = np.zeros(len(df))
    global_mean = df[target].mean()
    
    for train_idx, val_idx in kf.split(df):
        train, val = df.iloc[train_idx], df.iloc[val_idx]
        means = train.groupby(col)[target].mean()
        # Regularize with global mean
        encoded[val_idx] = val[col].map(means).fillna(global_mean)
    
    return encoded

df['color_encoded'] = target_encode(df, 'color', 'target')

# === Count Encoding ===
counts = df['city'].value_counts()
df['city_count'] = df['city'].map(counts)

# === Hash Encoding ===
def hash_encode(s, n_dims=20):
    return np.array([
        hash(f'{val}_{i}') % n_dims
        for val in s for i in range(n_dims)
    ]).reshape(-1, n_dims)

sections.title: Handling Missing Data
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Real data is always missing. The goal is to impute values without introducing bias or leaking information.

Strategies:

  • Drop: Drop rows (< 5% missing, MCAR) or columns (> 50% missing). Simple but loses information.
  • Mean/Median Imputation: Replace with column mean (normal distribution) or median (skewed). Can distort variance and covariance. Use train set statistics only — no future leakage.
  • Model-Based Imputation: KNN Imputer (weighted avg of k nearest neighbors), Iterative Imputer (MICE — each feature predicted from others iteratively). More accurate but computationally expensive.
  • Indicator + Impute: Add a binary column "was_missing" alongside the imputed value. Lets the model learn whether missingness is informative.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: 10K rows, "income" column has 30% missing. Mean income = 50K. Imputing 50K for all missing rows shrinks variance artificially. Better: MICE imputation or indicator + median.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer, KNNImputer, IterativeImputer
import pandas as pd

# === Simple Imputation (Mean/Median/Mode) ===
num_imputer = SimpleImputer(strategy='median')  # robust to outliers
cat_imputer = SimpleImputer(strategy='most_frequent')

df[['age', 'income']] = num_imputer.fit_transform(df[['age', 'income']])
df['city'] = cat_imputer.fit_transform(df[['city']]).ravel()

# === Indicator + Impute (preserve missingness signal) ===
df['income_missing'] = df['income'].isna().astype(int)
df['income'] = num_imputer.fit_transform(df[['income']])

# === KNN Imputer (uses feature similarity) ===
knn_imputer = KNNImputer(n_neighbors=5, weights='distance')
df_imputed = knn_imputer.fit_transform(df[['age', 'income', 'education_yrs']])

# === Iterative Imputer (MICE) ===
mice = IterativeImputer(max_iter=10, random_state=42)
df_mice = mice.fit_transform(df[['age', 'income', 'education_yrs']])

# === Missing indicator for tree models ===
# Trees can split on NaN natively (XGBoost, LightGBM)
# They learn the optimal direction for missing values

sections.title: Feature Scaling & Normalization
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Distance-based models (SVM, KNN, PCA) assume all features have the same scale. Scaling ensures one feature doesn't dominate just because of its units (e.g., income in dollars vs age in years).

Scaling methods:

  • Standardization (Z-score): z = (x - μ) / σ. Output has mean 0, variance 1. Default choice for most models. Robust to skewed data if median/IQR variant is used.
  • Min-Max Normalization: (x - min) / (max - min). Output in [0, 1]. Sensitive to outliers (single extreme value compresses everything). Use when you need bounded values.
  • Robust Scaling: (x - median) / (Q3 - Q1). Uses median and IQR. Not affected by outliers. Best for data with many outliers.
  • Power Transformations: Box-Cox (positive data only: y = (x^λ - 1)/λ) and Yeo-Johnson (any sign). Makes data more Gaussian. Helps linear models with skewed features.

[Practical Workflow] Critical rule: Fit scaler on training data only. Transform val/test using the same scaler. Never use test statistics to scale.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, MinMaxScaler, RobustScaler, PowerTransformer
import numpy as np

# === Standardization (most common) ===
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)  # same μ, σ from train

# === Min-Max ===
mm_scaler = MinMaxScaler()
X_train_mm = mm_scaler.fit_transform(X_train)

# === Robust (outlier-immune) ===
robust = RobustScaler()
X_train_robust = robust.fit_transform(X_train)

# === Power Transform ===
pt = PowerTransformer(method='yeo-johnson')  # works for negative values
X_train_power = pt.fit_transform(X_train)

# === Why scaling matters: SVM without scaling ===
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline

# Without scaling: income (0-200K) dominates age (0-100)
pipe = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), SVC(kernel='rbf'))
pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)

sections.title: Feature Crosses & Interactions
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A linear model can't learn "coffee sales go up when temperature drops" unless you give it temperature × coffee_price as an explicit feature. Crosses let linear models learn nonlinear relationships.

Types of interactions:

  • Numeric × Numeric: Multiply two features. Use domain knowledge (e.g., price × quantity = revenue). Add both original features as well — the model can zero out interaction if unhelpful.
  • Categorical × Numeric: One-hot encode category, multiply each binary column by the numeric feature. Creates different slopes per category.
  • Categorical × Categorical: Concatenate category values (e.g., "city_occupation"). Every unique pair gets its own weight. Explodes feature space — use only for high-signal pairs.
  • Polynomial Features: Degree-2 includes x₁², x₂², x₁x₂. Degree-3 includes x₁³, x₁²x₂, x₁x₂², x₂³. Explodes combinatorially — use with regularization.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete example: Fraud detection. Transaction amount + country alone is weak. Amount × country captures "$1000 in US is normal, $1000 in Nigeria is suspicious." AUC jumps from 0.72 to 0.89.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures, SplineTransformer
import numpy as np

# === Manual Interaction ===
df['age_education'] = df['age'] * df['education_years']

# === Polynomial Features (degree=2) ===
poly = PolynomialFeatures(degree=2, interaction_only=False, include_bias=False)
X_poly = poly.fit_transform(X)
# Features: x1, x2, x1^2, x2^2, x1*x2
print(poly.get_feature_names_out())

# === Interaction only (no squares) ===
poly_int = PolynomialFeatures(degree=2, interaction_only=True, include_bias=False)
X_interact = poly_int.fit_transform(X)
# Features: x1, x2, x1*x2

# === Categorical × Numeric ===
for cat_val in df['city'].unique():
    df[f'income_{cat_val}'] = df['income'] * (df['city'] == cat_val)

# === Spline Features (nonlinear basis expansion) ===
spline = SplineTransformer(degree=3, n_knots=5)
X_spline = spline.fit_transform(X)
# Each feature is replaced by 5+3-1 = 7 basis functions
# Allows flexible nonlinear fits without global polynomial

sections.title: Numerical Transformations & Binning
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Not all relationships are linear. Log transform handles multiplicative effects (price, income). Binning lets the model learn arbitrary step functions. Splines give smooth nonlinearity.

Key transformations:

  • Log Transform: x' = log(x + c). Compresses right skew. Turns multiplicative effects additive. Use for income, price, population. Add 1 if x can be 0 (log1p).
  • Box-Cox: x' = (x^λ - 1)/λ for λ ≠ 0, log(x) for λ = 0. Finds optimal power transform automatically. Requires positive x.
  • Binning (Discretization): Equal-width, equal-frequency, or quantile bins. Each bin becomes a one-hot feature. Removes fine-grained information but can capture nonlinear effects. Use for age, income brackets.
  • Rank/Percentile: Replace value with its rank or percentile rank. Robust to outliers. Useful for ensemble diversity.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import KBinsDiscretizer, FunctionTransformer

# === Log Transform ===
df['log_income'] = np.log1p(df['income'])  # log(1 + x)

# === Box-Cox (automatic optimal power) ===
from sklearn.preprocessing import PowerTransformer
pt = PowerTransformer(method='box-cox')  # requires positive data
df['income_boxcox'] = pt.fit_transform(df[['income']])

# === Binning ===
# Equal-width bins
binner = KBinsDiscretizer(
    n_bins=5, encode='onehot-dense', strategy='uniform'
)
age_bins = binner.fit_transform(df[['age']])

# Quantile bins (equal frequency)
qbinner = KBinsDiscretizer(
    n_bins=10, encode='onehot-dense', strategy='quantile'
)
df_quantile = qbinner.fit_transform(df[['income']])

# === Rank-based ===
from scipy.stats import rankdata
df['income_rank'] = rankdata(df['income']) / len(df)  # percentile

# === Custom FunctionTransformer ===
transformer = FunctionTransformer(np.log1p, validate=True)
df['log_feat'] = transformer.fit_transform(df[['skewed_feat']])

sections.title: Feature Selection Methods
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: More features aren't always better — irrelevant features add noise, increase overfitting, and slow training. Feature selection removes them.

Three families:

  • Filter Methods: Score each feature independently of the model. Fast but ignores feature interactions.
    • Variance Threshold: Remove constant/near-constant features (e.g., 99% same value). Simple first pass.
    • Correlation: Remove one of any pair with r > 0.95 (multicollinearity). Useful for linear models.
    • Mutual Information: Measures any dependency (linear + nonlinear). Works for both classification and regression. Higher = more informative.
    • Chi-Squared: For categorical features and categorical target. Tests independence.
  • Wrapper Methods: Train model on subsets, pick best subset. Expensive but accounts for interactions.
    • Forward Selection: Start empty, add best feature one at a time until no improvement.
    • Backward Elimination: Start with all, remove least important one at a time.
    • RFE (Recursive Feature Elimination): Train model, rank features by importance, drop bottom N, repeat. RFECV uses CV to find optimal number.
  • Embedded Methods: Selection is built into model training. Best of both worlds.
    • L1 Regularization: Lasso drives coefficients to exactly zero. The λ parameter controls sparsity.
    • Tree Importance: Random Forest / XGBoost feature importance (gain, split count, permutation).
    • SHAP: Model-agnostic feature importance with game-theoretic guarantees. Computationally expensive.

[Practical Workflow] Recommended pipeline: (1) Variance threshold to remove constants. (2) Correlation filter to handle multicollinearity. (3) Lasso or tree-based selection for final set. (4) Validate with RFECV on a held-out set.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.feature_selection import (
    VarianceThreshold, SelectKBest, chi2, mutual_info_classif,
    RFE, RFECV, SelectFromModel
)
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# === Variance Threshold ===
selector = VarianceThreshold(threshold=0.01)  # drop near-constant
X_reduced = selector.fit_transform(X)

# === Mutual Information ===
mi_scores = mutual_info_classif(X, y, discrete_features='auto')
feature_importance = pd.Series(mi_scores, index=feature_names).sort_values()

# === Chi-Squared (categorical features) ===
chi_selector = SelectKBest(chi2, k=20)
X_chi = chi_selector.fit_transform(X, y)

# === RFE with Logistic Regression ===
estimator = LogisticRegression(penalty='l2', max_iter=1000)
rfe = RFE(estimator, n_features_to_select=15)
rfe.fit(X, y)
selected = np.where(rfe.support_)[0]

# === RFECV (automatic optimal count) ===
rfecv = RFECV(
    estimator=RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100),
    cv=5, scoring='accuracy',
    min_features_to_select=5
)
rfecv.fit(X, y)
print(f'Optimal features: {rfecv.n_features_}')

# === L1 (embedded) ===
lasso = LogisticRegression(penalty='l1', C=0.1, solver='saga')
lasso.fit(X, y)
print(f'Non-zero coefs: {np.sum(lasso.coef_[0] != 0)}')

sections.title: Text Feature Extraction
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Raw text is unstructured. The goal is to convert text into fixed-length numerical vectors that preserve semantic information.

Text extraction methods:

  • Bag of Words (CountVectorizer): Each document → vector of word counts. Vocabulary size = N most frequent words. Simple but loses word order and semantics. Stop word removal and stemming help.
  • TF-IDF: TF(t,d) × IDF(t) where IDF = log(N / df(t)). TF = term frequency in document. IDF down-weights words that appear in many documents (like 'the', 'a'). Better than raw counts for most tasks.
  • N-Grams: Use phrases of N words (2-grams: 'not good', 'very bad'). Captures negations and multi-word expressions. Explodes feature space — use with hashing.
  • Hash Vectorizer: Use hashing trick to map to fixed dims. No need to maintain vocabulary. Useful for online/streaming settings. Collisions are acceptable if dim is large enough.

[Practical Workflow] Decision rule: Small datasets (< 10K docs) → TF-IDF with 1-3 grams. Large datasets → Hash vectorizer or learned embeddings. For trees: binary counts often work better than TF-IDF. For linear models: TF-IDF with normalization is standard.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import (
    CountVectorizer, TfidfVectorizer, HashingVectorizer
)
import pandas as pd

# === Bag of Words (top 5000 words) ===
bow = CountVectorizer(
    max_features=5000,
    stop_words='english',
    ngram_range=(1, 2),
    min_df=5  # ignore words in <5 docs
)
X_bow = bow.fit_transform(documents)
print(f'BoW shape: {X_bow.shape}')

# === TF-IDF (standard baseline) ===
tfidf = TfidfVectorizer(
    max_features=10000,
    stop_words='english',
    ngram_range=(1, 2),
    sublinear_tf=True,  # use 1 + log(tf)
    use_idf=True
)
X_tfidf = tfidf.fit_transform(documents)

# === Hashing (no vocabulary needed, fixed dims) ===
hash_vec = HashingVectorizer(
    n_features=2**18,
    stop_words='english',
    ngram_range=(1, 2),
    alternate_sign=False
)
X_hash = hash_vec.fit_transform(documents)

# === Interpret results ===
# Show top TF-IDF terms per document
feature_names = tfidf.get_feature_names_out()
doc_idx = 0
sorted_indices = X_tfidf[doc_idx].toarray().argsort()[0, -5:][::-1]
top_terms = [feature_names[i] for i in sorted_indices]
print(f'Top terms for doc {doc_idx}: {top_terms}')

sections.title: Time-Based & Lag Features
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Time gives structure. Past values predict future values. Extracting temporal features lets models learn trends, seasonality, and autoregressive patterns.

Time feature types:

  • Date Parts: Extract hour, day, month, year, dayofweek, quarter, is_weekend. Simple baseline that captures seasonality.
  • Cyclical Encoding: For cyclic features (hour, month), use sin(2πx/period) and cos(2πx/period). 23:00 and 01:00 are close in real life — raw encoding makes them far apart (23 vs 1). Sin/cos preserves this circular relationship.
  • Lag Features: x_{t-n} = value from n periods ago. Also: rolling mean, rolling std, difference (x_t - x_{t-1}), ratio (x_t / x_{t-1}). Choose lags based on domain (lag-7 for weekly patterns, lag-1 for daily).
  • Time Since: Days since last event, days since start, days until next holiday. Useful for event prediction.
sections.codeBlock:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# === Extract date parts ===
df['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'])
df['hour'] = df['timestamp'].dt.hour
df['dayofweek'] = df['timestamp'].dt.dayofweek
df['month'] = df['timestamp'].dt.month
df['quarter'] = df['timestamp'].dt.quarter
df['is_weekend'] = df['dayofweek'].isin([5, 6]).astype(int)

# === Cyclical encoding for hour ===
df['hour_sin'] = np.sin(2 * np.pi * df['hour'] / 24)
df['hour_cos'] = np.cos(2 * np.pi * df['hour'] / 24)

# === Lag features ===
df = df.sort_values('timestamp')
df['sales_lag_1'] = df['sales'].shift(1)
df['sales_lag_7'] = df['sales'].shift(7)
df['sales_rolling_7'] = df['sales'].rolling(7).mean()
df['sales_rolling_std_7'] = df['sales'].rolling(7).std()
df['sales_diff'] = df['sales'] - df['sales_lag_1']

# === Multi-step rolling features ===
for w in [3, 7, 14, 28]:
    df[f'sales_rolling_mean_{w}'] = df['sales'].rolling(w).mean()
    df[f'sales_rolling_max_{w}'] = df['sales'].rolling(w).max()
    df[f'sales_rolling_min_{w}'] = df['sales'].rolling(w).min()

# === Time since last event (e.g., promo) ===
promo_dates = df[df['is_promo'] == 1]['timestamp']
df['days_since_promo'] = (
    df['timestamp'].values[:, None] - promo_dates.values
).min(axis=1).astype('timedelta64[D]').astype(int)

# === Drop NaN rows from lag features ===
df = df.dropna()

sections.title: Feature Engineering Workflow & Pipeline
sections.description:
[Practical Workflow] Production pipeline: Feature engineering in production must be reproducible, consistent between train and serve, and immune to data leakage.

Best practices:

  • Use sklearn Pipelines: Combines all transforms into a single object. fit() on train, transform() on test. Eliminates data leakage between train and test.
  • ColumnTransformer: Apply different transforms to different columns: standardize numerics, one-hot encode categories, TF-IDF on text — all in one pipeline.
  • Feature Store: In production, use a feature store (Feast, Tecton) to define features once and serve them consistently for training and inference.
  • Versioning: Feature definitions change over time. Version your feature pipelines alongside model versions for reproducibility.

[Concrete Numbers] FAANG impact: At Uber, engineering features from GPS traces (pickup hour, dayofweek, distance to airport, historical driver ETA at this hour) improved ETA prediction by 40% — no model change needed.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

# === ColumnTransformer for mixed types ===
num_features = ['age', 'income', 'education_yrs']
cat_features = ['city', 'occupation']
text_features = ['description']

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(
    transformers=[
        ('num', StandardScaler(), num_features),
        ('cat', OneHotEncoder(drop='first', handle_unknown='infrequent_if_exist'), cat_features),
        ('text', TfidfVectorizer(max_features=1000), text_features),
    ]
)

# === Full pipeline ===
pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('preprocess', preprocessor),
    ('classifier', RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200))
])

# Fit once — applies all transforms
pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predict — applies same transforms automatically
preds = pipeline.predict(X_test)

# === Save pipeline for serving ===
import joblib
joblib.dump(pipeline, 'feature_pipeline.pkl')
